title: What Good Signage Is Worth
verdict: Signage and facade investment returns multiples in foot traffic that most landlords never bother to measure.
evidence: A controlled study of 60 retail corridors across six U.S. cities found that storefronts with professionally designed signage and cohesive facade treatments generated 15% to 25% more pedestrian stops than adjacent storefronts with standard or landlord-default signage. The effect was additive at the corridor level: blocks where more than half the storefronts had invested in design-forward signage saw overall foot traffic increases of 10% to 18% compared to similar blocks with inconsistent presentation. The cost of a professional signage and facade package, typically $8,000 to $25,000 per storefront, proved to be among the highest-ROI investments available to small retailers and their landlords.
frame: Most landlords treat signage as a tenant obligation and a lease exhibit, not as a strategic asset. This is a missed opportunity. The data suggests that coordinated signage and facade investment at the property or corridor level functions as a form of placemaking, signaling quality and intentionality to passersby in a way that drives measurable commercial outcomes. The smart approach is for landlords to fund or co-fund signage upgrades, enforce design standards through lease provisions, and treat the street-level presentation of their buildings as seriously as they treat the lobby. It is one of the few investments where the cost is trivial and the return is visible within a single lease term.
